key_points: Carbon is a versatile element that forms the basis of all living organisms and many things we use daily.
Covalent bonds are formed by the sharing of electrons between atoms, allowing them to achieve a stable noble gas configuration.
Carbon has a unique ability to form bonds with other atoms of carbon, giving rise to large molecules; this property is called Catenation.
Hydrocarbons are compounds made up of only hydrogen and carbon.
Saturated hydrocarbons (Alkanes) contain only single bonds, while Unsaturated hydrocarbons contain double (Alkenes) or triple (Alkynes) bonds.
examples: Allotropes of Carbon: Diamond (hardest substance), Graphite (good conductor), and Buckminsterfullerene ($C_{60}$).
Methane ($CH_4$): The simplest hydrocarbon, used widely as a fuel in CNG.
Saturated Compound: Ethane ($C_2H_6$) where all carbon atoms are linked by single bonds.
Unsaturated Compound: Ethene ($C_2H_4$) which contains a double bond.
Homologous Series: A series of compounds like Methane, Ethane, and Propane that have the same functional group.
exam_tips: Remember that Carbon is tetravalent, meaning it always forms four covalent bonds.
Note that Carbon cannot form $C^{4+}$ or $C^{4-}$ ions because of the high energy required, which is why it only shares electrons.
Identify that Graphite is an exception among non-metals as it conducts electricity due to free electrons.
Keep in mind that 'Catenation' and 'Tetravalency' are the two main reasons for the huge number of carbon compounds.
